title: L03 · Greetings & Verbs sein/haben/heißen
objective: Greet someone, say where you're from, introduce yourself. Memorise the three highest-frequency verbs in German: sein (to be), haben (to have), heißen (to be called).
strategy: These three verbs are irregular and appear in 80% of beginner sentences. Drill the conjugation table by tapping each row, then run 'Play section' for the example dialogues.

sections.title: Everyday greetings
sections.note: Memorise the formal vs informal split: 'Hallo' / 'Tschüss' are informal; 'Guten Tag' / 'Auf Wiedersehen' are neutral-formal.

blocks.hint: Standard polite greeting, used from late morning to evening.

blocks.translation: Good night! / ¡Buenas noches! (al irse a dormir)
Please. / Also: you're welcome. / Por favor. / De nada.
blocks.hint: Multipurpose: please, you're welcome, here you go, pardon?

sections.title: sein — 'to be' (the most important verb)
sections.note: Completely irregular. Memorise this table — you'll use it constantly.

blocks.rule: Irregular: ich BIN, du BIST, er IST, wir SIND, ihr SEID, sie SIND. There is no pattern — just memorise it.

examples.translation: He is a teacher. / Él es profesor.
They are from Germany. / Son de Alemania.

sections.title: haben — 'to have'
sections.note: Also irregular. After sein, this is the #2 verb you'll use.

blocks.rule: Irregular for du/er forms: du HAST, er HAT (loses the 'b'). The rest is regular.

examples.translation: Are you hungry? (lit. Do you have hunger?) / ¿Tienes hambre?
He has a car. / Él tiene un coche.
We have a dog. / Tenemos un perro.
They have three children. / Tienen tres hijos.

sections.title: heißen — 'to be called'
sections.note: Used in nearly every introduction. Notice the 'ß'.

blocks.rule: Regular except: stem ends in -ß, so the du form is 'heißt' (NOT 'heißst' — the ß already implies an s-sound).

examples.translation: I'm called Anna. / Me llamo Anna.
His name is Markus. / Él se llama Markus.

sections.title: A full introduction
sections.note: Listen to the whole dialogue first ('Play section'), then read along.

blocks.text: Hallo! Ich heiße Lukas. Wie heißt du?
blocks.audioText: Hallo! Ich heiße Lukas. Wie heißt du?
blocks.translation: Hi! I'm Lukas. What's your name? / ¡Hola! Me llamo Lukas. ¿Cómo te llamas?

blocks.text: Ich heiße Maria. Woher kommst du?
blocks.audioText: Ich heiße Maria. Woher kommst du?
blocks.translation: I'm Maria. Where are you from? / Me llamo María. ¿De dónde eres?

blocks.text: Ich komme aus Spanien. Ich wohne jetzt in Berlin.
blocks.audioText: Ich komme aus Spanien. Ich wohne jetzt in Berlin.
blocks.translation: I'm from Spain. I live in Berlin now. / Soy de España. Ahora vivo en Berlín.
Nice to meet you! / ¡Encantado de conocerte!
How old are you? / ¿Cuántos años tienes?
blocks.hint: German uses sein (to be) for age, not haben — 'Ich bin 25 Jahre alt.'

blocks.translation: I am thirty years old. / Tengo treinta años.
